Loss of Context — The Classic Bug
bug occurs when a method is extracted from its object and called later, without the original receiver.
bind at assignment: const fn = user.greet.bind(user);
Angular components are TypeScript classes. The
story inside a component is straightforward — but event binding and async callbacks require care.

Passing obj.method as a callback without bind — context is lost
Using a regular function inside setInterval/setTimeout expecting component this
Mixing arrow class fields with super.method() — arrows can't be overridden via prototype
Calling call/apply on an arrow function expecting to rebind it — it has no own this
Assuming this inside a nested object literal method refers to the outer class instance
How JavaScript Determines this — Step by Step

When a method is inherited via the prototype chain,
still refers to the actual instance (the object that was used to invoke the method), not the prototype where the method is defined.
bug occurs, the fastest debug strategy is:
Add console.log(this) at the top of the function to see the actual binding
Identify the call site — where in the code is this function being called?
Apply the priority rules to the call site to predict what this should be
If the call site is inside a framework (Angular change detection, RxJS), check whether it uses call/apply
